ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Matar Paneer specifically; mapped to the broader curry food product class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible FoodOn parent anchor. A specific Matar Paneer concept does not exist in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Matar Paneer; A0CKP (vegetable dishes) used as the closest defensible broader parent category. No more specific FoodEx2 code for Indian paneer-based curry exists as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Matar Paneer or equivalent Indian curry dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Matar Paneer is approximately 300 g, a typical restaurant or home serving size that provides a main-course portion of the curry alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN component data for paneer and green peas, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list Matar Paneer as a single composite entry. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for paneer (approximately 25–30% of dish weight) and green peas (approximately 20%), with tomato-onion gravy base (approximately 40%) and cooking fat (approximately 10%), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for paneer (FDC ID 173420) and green peas (FDC ID 170419) to validate macro plausibility. Input values from the original record (confidence 0.7) are retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA component ranges. Sodium value of 233 mg/100g reflects restaurant-level salting and is plausible for a restaurant preparation. ICMR-NIN is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is an approved secondary global source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on green peas GI from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Matar Paneer as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: green peas GI approximately 48 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021); paneer contributing negligible carbohydrate and lowering overall dish GI; tomato-onion gravy base with low intrinsic GI (approximately 35); and the moderating effect of fat (10 g/100g) and protein (6.67 g/100g) on glycaemic response. Weighted composite estimate yields GI approximately 45. GL per serving = (45 × 8.33 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 11.2 g per 300 g bowl.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 233 mg/100g and saturated fat 6.0 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 233 mg/100g contributes approximately 700 mg per standard 300 g bowl, approaching one-third of the 2000 mg/day WHO limit; combined with saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g exceeding the AHA 5 g/100g threshold for arterial stiffness, hypertensive individuals should request reduced salt, replacement of ghee with a small amount of vegetable oil, and omission or substitution of cream with low-fat yogurt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45), moderate carbohydrate 8.33 g/100g, dietary fibre 1.67 g/100g from green peas
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of approximately 45, carbohydrates of 8.33 g/100g, and dietary fibre from green peas moderating postprandial glucose rise, Matar Paneer is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without large portions of high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the protein content (6.67 g/100g) from paneer further blunts glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g and trans fat 0.17 g/100g from ghee and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g and trans fat at 0.17 g/100g from ghee and cream raise LDL-cholesterol concern for cardiovascular disease patients; individuals with CVD should request preparation with vegetable oil instead of ghee, omission of cream, and use of low-fat paneer to reduce saturated fat below 3.5 g/100g per AHA dietary guidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g may promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA; request replacement of ghee with extra-virgin olive oil and omission of cream; turmeric and ginger in the spice blend are anti-inflammatory and should be retained; RA patients sensitive to solanine should monitor symptoms from tomato and chilli powder.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matar Paneer is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry dish consisting of green peas (matar) and cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) simmered in a rich, spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish originates from the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent and is one of the most widely consumed vegetarian curries across South Asia and the Indian diaspora globally, including in Singapore where it is served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The gravy is built by sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in ghee or oil, then adding tomatoes, turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, and garam masala to create a deeply aromatic, mildly spiced sauce. Paneer contributes a soft, milky, slightly chewy texture and is the primary protein source, while green peas add sweetness, colour, and dietary fibre. Cream or yogurt is sometimes stirred in at the end for richness. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 6.67g protein, 10g fat (of which 6g is saturated from paneer and ghee), 8.33g carbohydrate, 1.67g dietary fibre, and 233mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 45 (low), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when not paired with large portions of high-GI staples. The high saturated fat content warrants caution for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. Matar Paneer is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal. It is typically served with roti, naan, paratha, or steamed basmati rice as a main course.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for paneer and green peas cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, as no single composite Matar Paneer entry exists in any approved primary source. Both ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy are approved sources per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Input values from the original record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) are retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA component ranges. GI is an analogous estimate based on green peas GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Cultural metadata in the input was incorrect (listed as Turkish cuisine with Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma ethnic tags and Singapore as country of origin); these have been corrected to North Indian cuisine with Indian subcontinent origin.
notes: 1. Cultural metadata correction: The input record incorrectly assigned primary_cuisine as 'Turkish' with ethnic_tags ['Turkish', 'Kebab', 'Shawarma'] and country_of_origin as 'Singapore'. Matar Paneer is a North Indian dish originating from the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent. These fields have been corrected accordingly. 2. Nutrient data: No single composite Matar Paneer entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for paneer and green peas (approved primary regional source for Indian dishes), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source; paneer FDC ID 173420, green peas FDC ID 170419). GrabFood delivery platform data has not been used as a primary nutrient source; input values are retained only where consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA component ranges. 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite restaurant-style preparation are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. Dual is_default portions in input: Both '1 bowl' (300g) and '1 plate' (350g) were flagged is_default=true in the input; '1 bowl' at 300g has been adopted as the canonical default serving for GL calculation. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Matar Paneer as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. Trans fat at 0.17g/100g is retained from input; this is plausible for ghee-containing preparations. 7. Ontology: No exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Matar Paneer; closest_parent category codes used with gap notes documented. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' per allowed values.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1–2: Corrected primary_cuisine from 'Turkish' to 'North Indian'; corrected region_of_origin; removed erroneous ethnic_tags; added mandatory local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); populated aliases_by_language; added GI estimate with University of Sydney GI Database analogous basis; calculated glycemic_load_per_serving; replaced health_suitability with health_context including Type 2 Diabetes, Hypertension, Cardiovascular Disease; documented all -1 fields; added ontology section; populated llm_training.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Fixed g2 — nutrient_source organization now explicitly names both ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources; removed any implication that GrabFood is a primary nutrient source; nutrient_source URL updated to reference both approved source homepages. Fixed g5 — foodon_match_type changed from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; foodex2_match_type changed from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; both now use only allowed values from ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Updated gap notes to reflect 'closest_parent' terminology. Fixed q2 — nutrient_source now clearly states ICMR-NIN and USDA as the evidence basis, not GrabFood. Fixed q4 — gi_source organization explicitly names University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2021) as approved GI sources.
